epigraph.text: The Lord God took the man and put him in the Garden of Eden to work it and take care of it.

content.text:
In the previous section, we established formulas for rectangles and 
parallelograms. Now we derive area formulas for triangles and 
trapezoids by relating them to these fundamental shapes. The key 
principle is **decomposition**: complex figures can be 
understood by breaking them into simpler pieces.
Area of a Triangle

Every triangle is exactly half of a parallelogram. This beautiful 
relationship gives us the triangle area formula.

content.title: Base and Height of a Triangle
content.content:
In a triangle:

- The **base** is any side of 
 the triangle.

- The **height** (or altitude) 
 is the perpendicular distance from the opposite vertex to the 
 line containing the base.

content.text:
**Deriving the formula:** Take any triangle $ABC$ with base 
$b$ and height $h$. Create a copy of the triangle, rotate it 180°, 
and attach it to form a parallelogram.

The two triangles together form a parallelogram with base $b$ and 
height $h$. Since the parallelogram has area $bh$, and the original 
triangle is exactly half of it:

content.title: Triangle Area
content.content:
The area of a triangle with base $b$ and height $h$ is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}bh
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding triangle area

content.text: Any Side Can Be the Base

Just like parallelograms, any side of a triangle can serve as the 
base. Each side has a corresponding height (the perpendicular 
distance to the opposite vertex). The area is the same regardless 
of which base-height pair you use.

content.title: Using different bases
content.problem:
Triangle $ABC$ has sides $AB = 15$, $BC = 12$, and $CA = 9$. 
The height from $C$ to side $AB$ is 7.2. Find the height 
from $A$ to side $BC$.
content.solution:
First, find the area using base $AB = 15$ and height 7.2:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}(15)(7.2) = 54
\]
Now use this area with base $BC = 12$ to find the height $h$:
\[
 54 = \frac{1}{2}(12)(h) \implies 54 = 6h \implies h = 9
\]
The height from $A$ to side $BC$ is 9 units.

content.text: Special Case: Right Triangles

For right triangles, finding the area is especially easy. The two 
legs are perpendicular to each other, so one leg serves as the 
base and the other as the height.

content.title: Right Triangle Area
content.content:
The area of a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$ is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}ab
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Right triangle area
content.problem: Find the area of a right triangle with legs 8 and 15.

content.text: Area of a Trapezoid

A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides, called the 
**bases**. Like the triangle, the trapezoid area formula 
comes from relating it to a parallelogram.

content.title: Trapezoid Parts
content.content:
In a trapezoid:

- The two parallel sides are called **bases**, 
 denoted $b_1$ and $b_2$.

- The **height** is the 
 perpendicular distance between the bases.

- The non-parallel sides are called **legs**.

content.text:
**Deriving the formula:** Take a trapezoid with bases $b_1$ 
and $b_2$ and height $h$. Create a copy, rotate it 180°, and 
attach it to form a parallelogram.

The resulting parallelogram has base $(b_1 + b_2)$ and height $h$, 
so its area is $(b_1 + b_2)h$. Since the parallelogram consists of 
two congruent trapezoids, each trapezoid has half this area:

content.title: Trapezoid Area
content.content:
The area of a trapezoid with bases $b_1$ and $b_2$ and height $h$ is:
\[
 A = \frac{1}{2}(b_1 + b_2)h
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Notice that $(b_1 + b_2)/2$ is the *average* of the two 
bases. So the trapezoid area formula can be thought of as:
\[
 A = (\text{average base}) \times h
\]

content.title: Trapezoid area
Finding a missing base
content.problem:
A trapezoid has area 120 m², height 10 m, and one base of 
length 8 m. Find the other base.

content.text: Isosceles Trapezoids

An **isosceles trapezoid** has legs of equal length. Its 
diagonals are also equal, and it has a line of symmetry through 
the midpoints of its bases.

content.title: Isosceles trapezoid
content.problem:
An isosceles trapezoid has bases 10 and 18, with legs of length 5. 
Find its height and area.

content.text: Special Cases and Connections

The trapezoid formula $A = \frac{1}{2}(b_1 + b_2)h$ is remarkably 
versatile. It actually includes other formulas as special cases:

items:
If $b_1 = b_2 = b$ (a parallelogram): 
 $A = \frac{1}{2}(b + b)h = bh$ ✓

content.text: Applications

content.title: Sail area
content.problem:
A triangular sail has a base of 8 feet along the boom and a 
height of 15 feet. How many square feet of canvas are needed 
to make the sail?

content.title: Cross-section of a channel
content.problem:
A drainage channel has a trapezoidal cross-section. The bottom 
is 2 meters wide, the top is 5 meters wide, and the depth is 
1.5 meters. Find the cross-sectional area.

content.text: Exercises

marginNotes.text:
By wisdom a house is built, and 
through understanding it is established; through knowledge its 
rooms are filled with rare and beautiful treasures.

How many are your works, Lord! In 
wisdom you made them all; the earth is full of your creatures.

Each frame is to be ten cubits long 
and a cubit and a half wide.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the 
wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and 
his paths beyond tracing out!
